schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the law of leprosy: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron, now to give the long law of leprosy (the plague in man, ch. 13, and the cleansing, ch. 14, the speaking, God's command, the law of leprosy, the to Moses and Aaron, both the lawgiver and the high priest, for the priests were to judge the plague, the occasion, after the laws of purification, now the law of leprosy, the great figure of sin and its defilement); the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron (the priests to judge the leprosy, the law given to both) — the new law (the law of leprosy, the priests' office of discernment); the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron — the law of leprosy introduced (the providence, God giving the law of leprosy, the figure of sin, the priests to judge) — Henry, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the law of leprosy
God giving the law of leprosy, the great figure of sin (chs. 13-14)
the priests to judge between clean and unclean

schools.summary: The plague brought to the priest: 'when a man shall have in the skin of his flesh a rising, a scab, or bright spot, and it be in the skin of his flesh like the plague of leprosy; then he shall be brought unto Aaron the priest, or unto one of his sons the priests' — the leprosy and the priest — 'then he shall be brought unto Aaron the priest, or unto one of his sons the priests': when a suspicious mark appeared (a rising, scab, or bright spot like leprosy), the man brought to the priest to be examined and judged (the witnesses noting the significance — the priest the judge of the plague, not the physician, but the priest, for leprosy was a ceremonial and religious matter, a figure of sin and its defilement, the lesson — leprosy the great Old Testament figure of sin, the loathsome, defiling, spreading, separating disease, a picture of sin in the soul — its hidden beginnings, its corruption, its spreading defilement, its separation from God and his people, the leper unclean, cast out, requiring cleansing, the priest's office to discern and judge the plague, the figure of the discerning of sin, the bringing of the sinner to be judged, the figure — the man brought to the priest, as the sinner must be brought to Christ the great High Priest, who alone can discern and cleanse the leprosy of sin, the lepers brought to Christ, Matthew 8:2-3; Mark 1:40-42; Luke 17:12-14, the cleansing only he can give); the various marks (the rising, scab, bright spot, the suspicious signs to be examined) — the figure (the marks of sin, to be discerned); brought unto the priest — the plague judged by the priest (the providence, leprosy the figure of sin, the priest the judge, the sinner brought to be discerned) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does leprosy and its bringing to the priest signify?

sides.position: Leprosy the great figure of sin (the loathsome, defiling, spreading, separating disease, a picture of sin in the soul), and the priest the judge of the plague (the discerning of sin) - the man brought to the priest as the sinner must be brought to Christ the great High Priest who alone can cleanse the leprosy of sin (Matthew 8:2-3; Mark 1:40-42; Luke 17:12-14)

themes: When a man shall have in the skin of his flesh a rising, a scab, or bright spot... like the plague of leprosy; then he shall be brought unto Aaron the priest
the plague brought to the priest (the crux of leprosy as the figure of sin)
leprosy the great figure of sin, the priest the judge of the plague
the sinner brought as to Christ the great High Priest who alone can cleanse (Matthew 8:2-3; Luke 17:12-14)

schools.summary: The marks of the plague: 'and the priest shall look on the plague in the skin of the flesh: and when the hair in the plague is turned white, and the plague in sight be deeper than the skin of his flesh, it is a plague of leprosy: and the priest shall look on him, and pronounce him unclean' — the signs — 'the hair in the plague is turned white, and the plague... be deeper than the skin': two marks revealing true leprosy — the hair turned white (a sign of the deep, settled disease) and the plague deeper than the skin (the corruption reaching below the surface, the witnesses noting the significance — the deep and inward corruption, the leprosy not a surface blemish but a deep-rooted disease, the corruption reaching below the skin, the marks of the settled plague, the lesson — sin's deep and inward nature, sin not a surface stain but a deep corruption of the heart, reaching below the surface, the inward defilement, 'the whole head is sick, and the whole heart faint... from the sole of the foot even unto the head there is no soundness,' Isaiah 1:5-6, the deceitfulness and depth of sin, Jeremiah 17:9, the priest's judgment, pronouncing unclean, the figure of the verdict of God's law on sin, the figure — the depth of the corruption that only the great High Priest can cleanse); the priest's careful examination (looking on the plague, judging by the marks) — the discernment (the careful judgment of the plague, the figure of the discerning of sin); 'the priest shall look on him, and pronounce him unclean' (the priest pronouncing unclean, the verdict on the plague, the figure, the verdict of God's law on sin, the sinner pronounced unclean, Romans 3:19, the providence, the unclean pronounced, the figure of sin judged) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What do the marks of the plague signify?

sides.position: The hair turned white and the plague deeper than the skin, marks of the deep, settled disease - a figure of sin's deep and inward nature (not a surface stain but a deep corruption of the heart, Isaiah 1:5-6; Jeremiah 17:9), the priest pronouncing unclean a figure of the verdict of God's law on sin (Romans 3:19)

themes: When the hair in the plague is turned white, and the plague in sight be deeper than the skin of his flesh, it is a plague of leprosy: and the priest shall... pronounce him unclean
the marks of the plague (the crux of sin's deep and inward corruption)
the hair white and the plague deeper than the skin, marks of the deep, settled disease
a figure of sin's deep inward nature, the verdict of God's law (Isaiah 1:5-6; Jeremiah 17:9; Romans 3:19)

schools.summary: The shutting up of the doubtful for seven days: 'if the bright spot be white in the skin of his flesh, and in sight be not deeper than the skin, and the hair thereof be not turned white; then the priest shall shut up him that hath the plague seven days' — if the marks were doubtful (the spot not deeper than the skin, the hair not white), the priest shutting the man up for seven days, to watch the development (the doubtful case, the marks uncertain, not yet clearly leprosy, the shutting up, the man isolated, watched for seven days, the figure, the careful judgment, the time of watching, the not hasty condemning nor hasty clearing, the patient discernment); the doubtful marks (not deeper, not white, the case uncertain) — the discernment (the careful judgment, not hasty); 'the priest shall shut up him that hath the plague seven days' (the man shut up, watched, the time of testing, the careful judgment, the figure, the patient discernment, the time of watching before the verdict, the providence, the doubtful watched, the verdict deferred, vv.5-6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: If the bright spot be white in the skin of his flesh, and in sight be not deeper than the skin, and the hair thereof be not turned white; then the priest shall shut up him that hath the plague seven days
the shutting up of the doubtful for seven days
the doubtful case watched, the careful judgment, not hasty
the patient discernment, the time of watching before the verdict

schools.summary: The re-examination on the seventh day: 'and the priest shall look on him the seventh day: and, behold, if the plague in his sight be at a stay, and the plague spread not in the skin; then the priest shall shut him up seven days more' — on the seventh day the priest examining again, and if the plague had stayed (not spread), the man being shut up another seven days (the re-examination, the priest looking again, the at a stay, the plague not spreading, the doubtful still uncertain, the seven days more, a second period of watching, the figure, the spreading the key sign of the plague, the careful, repeated judgment); the plague not spreading (the key sign, the spreading marking the true leprosy) — the discernment (the spreading the test); 'then the priest shall shut him up seven days more' (the second period of watching, the careful, patient judgment, the figure, the repeated discernment, the watching of the spreading, sin known by its spreading, the small corruption that spreads, James 1:14-15, the providence, the doubtful watched again, the verdict still deferred, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the priest shall look on him the seventh day: and, behold, if the plague in his sight be at a stay, and the plague spread not in the skin; then the priest shall shut him up seven days more
the re-examination on the seventh day
the plague watched for spreading, the careful repeated discernment
sin known by its spreading, the small corruption that spreads (James 1:14-15)

schools.summary: The clean pronouncement for the faded, non-spreading mark: 'and the priest shall look on him again the seventh day: and, behold, if the plague be somewhat dark, and the plague spread not in the skin, the priest shall pronounce him clean: it is but a scab: and he shall wash his clothes, and be clean' — on the second seventh day, if the plague had faded (somewhat dark, dimmer) and not spread, the priest pronouncing the man clean — it was only a scab, not leprosy (the faded and not spreading, the marks of the harmless scab, not the spreading leprosy, the pronounce clean, the verdict of cleanness, the wash his clothes, the cleansing, the figure, the harmless blemish distinguished from the deadly plague, not every spot is leprosy, the careful discernment that does not condemn the clean); the plague faded and not spreading (the marks of cleanness, the scab) — the discernment (the harmless distinguished from the deadly); 'pronounce him clean: it is but a scab: and he shall wash his clothes, and be clean' (the clean pronounced, the clothes washed, the figure, the harmless blemish, the cleansing, the discernment that distinguishes infirmity from deadly sin, the providence, the clean pronounced, the man restored) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: If the plague be somewhat dark, and the plague spread not in the skin, the priest shall pronounce him clean: it is but a scab
the clean pronouncement for the faded, non-spreading mark
the harmless blemish distinguished from the deadly plague, not every spot is leprosy
the careful discernment that does not condemn the clean

schools.summary: The spreading scab after the cleansing: 'but if the scab spread much abroad in the skin, after that he hath been seen of the priest for his cleansing, he shall be seen of the priest again' — if, after being pronounced clean, the scab spread, the man to be seen by the priest again (the spreading after cleansing, the disease breaking out again, the apparent cleanness proving false, the seen again, the re-examination, the verdict reconsidered, the figure, the spreading the mark of the true plague, the sin that breaks out again, the false peace, the corruption that proves itself by spreading, the need of fresh examination); the scab spreading (the disease proving itself by spreading, even after the apparent cleanness) — the figure (the spreading the test, the corruption that breaks out); 'he shall be seen of the priest again' (the man re-examined, the verdict reconsidered, the spreading judged, the figure, the fresh examination, the sin that proves itself by spreading, the providence, the spreading judged, the unclean to be pronounced, vv.8) — Gill.

themes: But if the scab spread much abroad in the skin, after that he hath been seen of the priest for his cleansing, he shall be seen of the priest again
the spreading scab after the cleansing
the disease breaking out after the apparent cleanness, the spreading the mark of the true plague
the sin that proves itself by spreading, the need of fresh examination

schools.summary: The unclean pronouncement for the spreading scab: 'and if the priest see that, behold, the scab spreadeth in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a leprosy' — if the priest saw the scab spreading, he pronouncing the man unclean — it was leprosy (the spreading, the decisive mark, the disease proving itself, the pronounce unclean, the verdict, the it is a leprosy, the true plague, confirmed by the spreading, the figure, the spreading the proof of the true plague, sin known by its spreading and growth, the corruption that does not stay but spreads, the verdict on the proven sin); the spreading the decisive sign (the disease proven by its spreading) — the figure (sin known by its spreading); 'the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a leprosy' (the unclean pronounced, the verdict on the spreading plague, the figure, the verdict on the proven sin, the corruption confirmed, the providence, the leprosy confirmed, the unclean pronounced) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Gill.

themes: And if the priest see that, behold, the scab spreadeth in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a leprosy
the unclean pronouncement for the spreading scab
the spreading the proof of the true plague, sin known by its spreading
the verdict on the confirmed corruption

schools.summary: The leper brought to the priest: 'when the plague of leprosy is in a man, then he shall be brought unto the priest' — when the plague of leprosy was in a man, he being brought to the priest to be judged (the plague in a man, the leprosy present, the brought to the priest, the man brought to be examined, vv.2, the figure, the sinner brought to the priest, as to Christ, to be judged and cleansed, the leprosy of sin brought to the great High Priest); the leprosy in the man (the disease present, to be judged) — the figure (the plague of sin in the soul); 'then he shall be brought unto the priest' (the man brought to the priest, to be judged, vv.2, the figure, the sinner brought to Christ the High Priest, Matthew 8:2, the providence, the leper brought, the verdict to follow, vv.10-11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When the plague of leprosy is in a man, then he shall be brought unto the priest
the leper brought to the priest
the leprosy of sin in the soul, the man brought to be judged (vv.2)
the sinner brought to Christ the great High Priest (Matthew 8:2)

schools.summary: The marks of the confirmed leprosy: 'and the priest shall see him: and, behold, if the rising be white in the skin, and it have turned the hair white, and there be quick raw flesh in the rising' — the priest looking for the marks of confirmed leprosy: the white rising, the hair turned white, and quick (living) raw flesh in the rising (the white rising and white hair, the marks of the settled disease, the quick raw flesh, the open, raw, living sore, the worst sign, the corruption breaking out, the figure, the marks of the confirmed plague, the raw flesh the open corruption, the living sin exposed); the white rising and white hair (the marks of leprosy, vv.3) — the signs (the confirmed disease); 'and there be quick raw flesh in the rising' (the quick raw flesh, the open, living sore, the worst mark, the corruption exposed, the figure, the raw, living corruption, the open sin, the worst sign, the living, unhealed wound, the providence, the marks of the confirmed leprosy, the verdict to follow, vv.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the priest shall see him: and, behold, if the rising be white in the skin, and it have turned the hair white, and there be quick raw flesh in the rising
the marks of the confirmed leprosy
the white rising and white hair, the marks of the settled disease (vv.3)
the quick raw flesh the open, living corruption, the figure of exposed sin

schools.summary: The old leprosy pronounced unclean without shutting up: 'it is an old leprosy in the skin of his flesh, and the priest shall pronounce him unclean, and shall not shut him up: for he is unclean' — this being an old (inveterate, settled, long-standing) leprosy, and the priest pronouncing the man unclean at once, without shutting him up, for he was clearly unclean (the old leprosy, the settled, chronic, manifest disease, the not shut up, no need to watch, the case clear, the pronounced unclean at once, the manifest plague needing no further testing, the figure, the old, settled, manifest sin, needing no inquiry, the confirmed corruption, the settled habit of sin, the hardened state); the old, inveterate leprosy (the settled, long-standing disease, manifest) — the figure (the old, settled, confirmed sin, the inveterate corruption); 'pronounce him unclean, and shall not shut him up: for he is unclean' (the immediate verdict, no watching needed, the manifest plague, the figure, the manifest, settled sin needing no inquiry, the open, confirmed corruption, the providence, the old leprosy pronounced unclean at once) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: It is an old leprosy in the skin of his flesh, and the priest shall pronounce him unclean, and shall not shut him up: for he is unclean
the old leprosy pronounced unclean without shutting up
the inveterate, settled, manifest disease needing no further testing
a figure of the old, settled, confirmed sin, the hardened corruption

schools.summary: The leprosy that covers the whole body: 'and if a leprosy break out abroad in the skin, and the leprosy cover all the skin of him that hath the plague from his head even to his foot, wheresoever the priest looketh' — if the leprosy broke out and covered the whole body, head to foot, wherever the priest looked (the breaking out abroad, the leprosy spreading over all, the cover all the skin, the whole body covered, head to foot, the figure, the leprosy that covers all, the strange case, the verdict to follow, vv.13, the figure of the sinner wholly covered with the acknowledgment of sin); the leprosy breaking out over all (the spreading covering the whole) — the figure (the disease covering all); 'cover all the skin of him that hath the plague from his head even to his foot' (the whole body covered, head to foot, the figure, the leprosy that covers all, the verdict, vv.13, the figure of the sinner wholly acknowledging his corruption, the providence, the whole covered, the strange clean verdict to follow, vv.13) — Gill.

themes: And if a leprosy break out abroad in the skin, and the leprosy cover all the skin of him that hath the plague from his head even to his foot
the leprosy that covers the whole body
the disease covering the whole body, head to foot, the strange case
the clean verdict to follow (vv.13)

schools.summary: The whole leprous pronounced clean: 'then the priest shall consider: and, behold, if the leprosy have covered all his flesh, he shall pronounce him clean that hath the plague: it is all turned white: he is clean' — the strange verdict — 'if the leprosy have covered all his flesh, he shall pronounce him clean': strangely, when the leprosy covered the whole body and all turned white, the man pronounced clean (the witnesses noting the meaning — the disease, having come fully to the surface and turned all white, the corruption fully out, the crisis past, the disease no longer active and spreading but spent, no longer ceremonially defiling, the lesson — the great spiritual paradox of confession, the figure that the one who is wholly covered, who acknowledges himself wholly corrupt and a sinner through and through, is pronounced clean — for he that confesses all his sin, hiding nothing, owning himself wholly leprous, is justified, 'if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins,' 1 John 1:9, the publican who said 'God be merciful to me a sinner' went down justified, Luke 18:13-14, the contrast with the self-righteous who hides his spots, the figure — the sinner who owns his whole corruption is cleansed, while the one who hides a spot of seeming soundness, the raw flesh, vv.14, is unclean, the cleansing of the one who confesses all); the all turned white pronounced clean (the disease come fully out, the man clean) — the figure (the one who owns himself wholly a sinner is cleansed, the confession of all sin, the lesson, the whole acknowledgment, the cleansing of the penitent); the whole covered pronounced clean — the paradox of confession (the providence, the figure of the sinner who confesses all, justified, 1 John 1:9) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: Why is the wholly leprous man pronounced clean?

sides.position: The disease fully out and spent, no longer defiling - a figure of the great spiritual paradox that the one who acknowledges himself wholly corrupt and a sinner through and through is pronounced clean (the confession of all sin, hiding nothing, 1 John 1:9; the publican justified, Luke 18:13-14), in contrast with the self-righteous who hides his spots and the raw flesh that defiles (vv.14)

themes: If the leprosy have covered all his flesh, he shall pronounce him clean that hath the plague: it is all turned white: he is clean
the whole leprous pronounced clean (the crux of the paradox of confession)
the one who owns himself wholly a sinner, confessing all, is pronounced clean (1 John 1:9; Luke 18:13-14)
in contrast with the self-righteous who hides his spots

schools.summary: The raw flesh that makes unclean: 'but when raw flesh appeareth in him, he shall be unclean' — the raw flesh — 'when raw flesh appeareth in him, he shall be unclean': in contrast with the wholly-white leper pronounced clean (vv.13), if raw (living, open) flesh appeared, the man unclean (the witnesses noting the meaning — the raw flesh, the open, living sore, the active corruption, the disease still alive and spreading, defiling, whereas the all-white, the disease spent, was clean, the lesson — the great spiritual contrast, the figure that living, unmortified, unconfessed corruption defiles — the one spot of 'raw flesh,' the unacknowledged sin, the hidden living corruption, makes unclean, even where there is much seeming whiteness, the figure — the contrast between the one who confesses all, clean, vv.13, and the one who harbors a living, unconfessed sin, unclean, the danger of the one spot kept back, the secret sin not owned, the living corruption hidden, the leaven that leavens, the spot that defiles, the partial confession that is no confession, the cleansing of those who confess all and the defilement of those who keep back a living sin, Acts 5:1-11, Ananias; Joshua 7, Achan's hidden sin); the raw, living flesh (the open sore, the active corruption) — the figure (the living, unmortified sin, the open corruption that defiles, the contrast, the living sin against the spent and confessed); 'he shall be unclean' (the unclean verdict, the living corruption defiling, the figure, the living, unconfessed sin defiles, in contrast with the confessed, the providence, the raw flesh unclean, the verdict, vv.15) — Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the raw flesh that defiles signify?

sides.position: The open, living sore (the active corruption) defiling in contrast with the spent, all-white disease (vv.13) - a figure that living, unmortified, unconfessed corruption defiles (the one spot of raw flesh, the unacknowledged sin, makes unclean amid much seeming whiteness), the danger of the one living sin kept back (Joshua 7; Acts 5:1-11)

themes: But when raw flesh appeareth in him, he shall be unclean
the raw flesh that makes unclean (the crux of the living corruption that defiles)
living, unmortified, unconfessed corruption defiles, in contrast with the spent and confessed (vv.13)
the danger of the one living sin kept back, the hidden corruption (Joshua 7; Acts 5:1-11)

schools.summary: The priest pronouncing the raw flesh unclean: 'and the priest shall see the raw flesh, and pronounce him to be unclean: for the raw flesh is unclean: it is a leprosy' — the priest, seeing the raw flesh, pronouncing the man unclean, for the raw flesh was unclean — it was leprosy (the seeing the raw flesh, the priest discerning the living sore, the pronounce unclean, the verdict on the living corruption, the it is a leprosy, the active, defiling disease, the figure, the verdict on the living, unconfessed sin, the raw flesh the mark of the active corruption, vv.14); the priest pronouncing unclean for the raw flesh (the living corruption, vv.14) — the figure (the verdict on the active sin); 'for the raw flesh is unclean: it is a leprosy' (the raw flesh the mark of the active leprosy, the living corruption defiling, the figure, the living, unmortified sin the active corruption, vv.14, the providence, the raw flesh judged, the unclean verdict) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the priest shall see the raw flesh, and pronounce him to be unclean: for the raw flesh is unclean: it is a leprosy
the priest pronouncing the raw flesh unclean
the priest's verdict on the living, defiling corruption (vv.14)
the figure of the active, unconfessed sin judged

schools.summary: The raw flesh turning white and the man coming to the priest: 'or if the raw flesh turn again, and be changed unto white, he shall come unto the priest' — if the raw flesh (the living sore, vv.14-15) turned again and changed to white, the man coming to the priest to be re-examined (the raw flesh turning white, the active corruption healing, turning to the white of the spent disease, the coming to the priest, the re-examination, the figure, the living corruption changed, the sore healed, the figure of the sin owned and the corruption brought to the surface, the change toward cleanness); the raw flesh turning white (the active sore healing, the change, vv.13) — the figure (the corruption changed toward cleanness); 'he shall come unto the priest' (the man coming to be re-examined, the verdict reconsidered, the figure, the change brought to the priest, the re-examination, the providence, the changed flesh examined, the clean verdict to follow, vv.17) — Gill.

themes: Or if the raw flesh turn again, and be changed unto white, he shall come unto the priest
the raw flesh turning white and the man coming to the priest
the living corruption healed, turned to the white of the spent disease
brought to the priest for the clean verdict (vv.13)

schools.summary: The clean pronouncement when the plague turns white: 'and the priest shall see him: and, behold, if the plague be turned into white; then the priest shall pronounce him clean that hath the plague: he is clean' — the priest examining, and if the plague had turned white (the raw flesh healed, all white), pronouncing the man clean (the plague turned white, the disease spent, the raw flesh healed, the pronounce clean, the verdict of cleanness, the figure, the corruption fully come out and spent, the man clean, as vv.13, the figure of the cleansing of the one whose corruption is owned and healed); the plague turned white (the raw flesh healed, the disease spent, vv.13) — the figure (the corruption spent, the cleanness); 'the priest shall pronounce him clean... he is clean' (the clean pronounced, the man restored, the figure, the cleansing of the owned and healed corruption, the providence, the clean verdict, the man restored) — Gill.

themes: And the priest shall see him: and, behold, if the plague be turned into white; then the priest shall pronounce him clean that hath the plague: he is clean
the clean pronouncement when the plague turns white
the corruption come fully out and spent, the man pronounced clean (vv.13)
the figure of the owned and healed corruption cleansed

schools.summary: The case of the healed boil: 'the flesh also, in which, even in the skin thereof, was a boil, and is healed' — the next case concerning a boil (a sore, an inflamed swelling) that had healed, but in whose place a leprous mark might arise (the boil, the inflamed sore, healed, the in the place of the boil, the leprosy might arise in the scar of the healed boil, vv.19-20, the figure, the old wound, the place of former trouble, where new corruption might break out, the danger of the healed-over place, the sin that returns where there was former affliction); the boil (the inflamed sore, the former affliction) — the case (the boil healed, the place watched); 'and is healed' (the boil healed, the sore closed, the figure, the healed-over place where new corruption might rise, the danger of the old wound, the providence, the healed boil, the leprous mark to be judged, vv.19-23) — Henry, Gill.

themes: The flesh also, in which, even in the skin thereof, was a boil, and is healed
the case of the healed boil
the healed sore in whose place a leprous mark might arise (vv.19-20)
the danger of the old wound where new corruption might break out

schools.summary: The white rising in the place of the boil: 'and in the place of the boil there be a white rising, or a bright spot, white, and somewhat reddish, and it be shewed to the priest' — if in the place of the healed boil there appeared a white rising or a bright reddish-white spot, it being shown to the priest (the white rising in the boil's place, the suspicious mark in the old scar, the somewhat reddish, the color of the mark, the shewed to the priest, the man bringing it to be judged, vv.2, the figure, the new mark in the old place, the corruption arising where there was former trouble, the sin breaking out in the old wound); the white rising in the boil's place (the suspicious mark in the old scar) — the case (the mark to be judged); 'white, and somewhat reddish, and it be shewed to the priest' (the mark shown to the priest, the man bringing it to be judged, the figure, the new corruption in the old place, brought to be discerned, the providence, the mark examined, the verdict to follow, vv.20) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And in the place of the boil there be a white rising, or a bright spot, white, and somewhat reddish, and it be shewed to the priest
the white rising in the place of the boil
the new mark in the old scar, the corruption arising where there was former trouble
the sin breaking out in the old wound, brought to be judged (vv.2)

schools.summary: The boil's mark judged to be leprosy: 'and if, when the priest seeth it, behold, it be in sight lower than the skin, and the hair thereof be turned white; the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a plague of leprosy broken out of the boil' — if the mark was lower than the skin (deeper) and the hair turned white, the priest pronouncing unclean — it was leprosy broken out of the boil (the lower than the skin and white hair, the marks of true leprosy, vv.3, the broken out of the boil, the leprosy arising from the old sore, the figure, the corruption breaking out of the old wound, the deep, settled plague in the old place, the sin that proves itself in the place of former trouble); the marks of leprosy (deeper than the skin, white hair, vv.3) — the signs (the true plague); 'pronounce him unclean: it is a plague of leprosy broken out of the boil' (the unclean verdict, the leprosy from the boil, the figure, the corruption breaking out of the old wound, the providence, the leprosy of the boil judged unclean) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And if, when the priest seeth it, behold, it be in sight lower than the skin, and the hair thereof be turned white; the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a plague of leprosy broken out of the boil
the boil's mark judged to be leprosy
the deep, settled plague arising from the old sore (vv.3)
the figure of the sin that proves itself in the place of former trouble

schools.summary: The doubtful boil shut up for seven days: 'but if the priest look on it, and, behold, there be no white hairs therein, and if it be not lower than the skin, but be somewhat dark; then the priest shall shut him up seven days' — if the marks were doubtful (no white hairs, not lower than the skin, somewhat dark), the priest shutting the man up seven days (the doubtful marks, the signs uncertain, vv.4, the shut up seven days, the time of watching, the figure, the careful judgment, the watching of the doubtful case, as before, vv.4-5); the doubtful marks (no white hair, not deeper, dark, vv.4) — the discernment (the careful judgment); 'the priest shall shut him up seven days' (the man watched, the time of testing, the figure, the patient discernment, vv.4-5, the providence, the doubtful watched, the verdict deferred, vv.22-23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But if the priest look on it, and, behold, there be no white hairs therein, and if it be not lower than the skin, but be somewhat dark; then the priest shall shut him up seven days

the verdict deferred

schools.summary: The spreading mark pronounced unclean: 'and if it spread much abroad in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a plague' — if the mark spread during the watching, the priest pronouncing unclean — it was a plague (the spreading, the decisive sign, vv.8, the pronounce unclean, the verdict, the it is a plague, the leprosy confirmed by spreading, the figure, the spreading the proof of the plague, sin known by its spreading); the spreading the decisive sign (the plague proven by spreading, vv.8) — the figure (sin known by its spreading); 'the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a plague' (the unclean verdict, the spreading plague, the figure, the verdict on the proven corruption, the providence, the leprosy confirmed, the unclean pronounced) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And if it spread much abroad in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a plague
the spreading mark pronounced unclean
the spreading the proof of the plague, sin known by its spreading (vv.8)
the verdict on the confirmed corruption

schools.summary: The non-spreading spot pronounced clean: 'but if the bright spot stay in his place, and spread not, it is a burning boil; and the priest shall pronounce him clean' — if the bright spot stayed and did not spread, it being only the scar of the burning boil (the inflammation), and the priest pronouncing clean (the stay and spread not, the marks of the harmless scar, not the spreading plague, the burning boil, the inflammation's scar, not leprosy, the pronounce clean, the verdict of cleanness, the figure, the harmless mark distinguished from the deadly plague, not every mark in the old wound is leprosy, the careful discernment); the spot not spreading (the harmless scar, vv.6) — the discernment (the harmless distinguished from the deadly); 'it is a burning boil; and the priest shall pronounce him clean' (the burning boil's scar, not leprosy, the clean verdict, the figure, the harmless mark, the cleansing, the discernment that does not condemn the clean, the providence, the clean pronounced, the man restored) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: But if the bright spot stay in his place, and spread not, it is a burning boil; and the priest shall pronounce him clean
the non-spreading spot pronounced clean
the harmless scar of the burning boil distinguished from the deadly plague (vv.6)
the clean not condemned, the careful discernment

schools.summary: The case of the burn: 'or if there be any flesh, in the skin whereof there is a hot burning, and the quick flesh that burneth have a white reddish, or white, bright spot' — the next case concerning a burn (a place burnt by fire), in whose raw flesh a white-reddish or white bright spot might appear (the hot burning, the burn by fire, the quick flesh that burneth, the raw burnt flesh, the white reddish bright spot, the suspicious mark in the burn, the figure, the burn, another old wound where leprosy might arise, the place of former injury watched, as the boil, vv.18); the burn by fire (the injury, the place watched) — the case (the burn, the mark to be judged); 'the quick flesh that burneth have a white reddish, or white, bright spot' (the suspicious mark in the burn, the bright spot, the leprosy that might arise, the figure, the corruption in the place of the burn, as in the boil, the providence, the burn's mark to be judged, vv.25) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Or if there be any flesh, in the skin whereof there is a hot burning, and the quick flesh that burneth have a white reddish, or white, bright spot
the case of the burn
the burn, another old wound where leprosy might arise (vv.18)
the place of former injury watched, the mark to be judged

schools.summary: The burn's mark judged to be leprosy: 'then the priest shall look upon it: and, behold, if the hair in the bright spot be turned white, and it be in sight deeper than the skin; it is a leprosy broken out of the burning: wherefore the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is the plague of leprosy' — if the hair turned white and the mark was deeper than the skin, it being leprosy broken out of the burn, and the priest pronouncing unclean (the white hair and deeper than the skin, the marks of leprosy, vv.3, the broken out of the burning, the leprosy arising from the burn, the figure, the corruption breaking out of the old burn, as the boil, the deep, settled plague in the place of injury); the marks of leprosy (white hair, deeper, vv.3) — the signs (the true plague); 'it is a leprosy broken out of the burning... pronounce him unclean: it is the plague of leprosy' (the leprosy from the burn, the unclean verdict, the figure, the corruption breaking out of the old injury, the providence, the leprosy of the burn judged unclean) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: If the hair in the bright spot be turned white, and it be in sight deeper than the skin; it is a leprosy broken out of the burning... the priest shall pronounce him unclean
the burn's mark judged to be leprosy
the deep, settled plague arising from the old burn (vv.3)
the figure of the sin that proves itself in the place of former injury

schools.summary: The doubtful burn shut up for seven days: 'but if the priest look on it, and, behold, there be no white hair in the bright spot, and it be no lower than the other skin, but be somewhat dark; then the priest shall shut him up seven days' — if the marks were doubtful (no white hair, not lower, somewhat dark), the priest shutting the man up seven days (the doubtful marks, the signs uncertain, vv.21, the shut up seven days, the time of watching, the figure, the careful judgment of the doubtful case, as before); the doubtful marks (no white hair, not lower, dark, vv.21) — the discernment (the careful judgment); 'the priest shall shut him up seven days' (the man watched, the time of testing, the figure, the patient discernment, the providence, the doubtful watched, the verdict deferred, vv.27-28) — JFB, Gill.

themes: But if the priest look on it, and, behold, there be no white hair in the bright spot, and it be no lower than the other skin, but be somewhat dark; then the priest shall shut him up seven days
the doubtful burn shut up for seven days
the doubtful case watched, the careful judgment (vv.21)
the verdict deferred

schools.summary: The spreading burn pronounced unclean: 'and the priest shall look upon him the seventh day: and if it be spread much abroad in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is the plague of leprosy' — on the seventh day, if the mark had spread, the priest pronouncing unclean — it was leprosy (the spreading, the decisive sign, vv.22, the pronounce unclean, the verdict, the figure, the spreading the proof of the plague, sin known by its spreading); the spreading the decisive sign (the plague proven, vv.22) — the figure (sin known by its spreading); 'pronounce him unclean: it is the plague of leprosy' (the unclean verdict, the spreading plague, the figure, the verdict on the proven corruption, the providence, the leprosy confirmed, the unclean pronounced) — Gill.

themes: And the priest shall look upon him the seventh day: and if it be spread much abroad in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is the plague of leprosy
the spreading burn pronounced unclean
the spreading the proof of the plague, sin known by its spreading (vv.22)
the verdict on the confirmed corruption

schools.summary: The non-spreading burn pronounced clean: 'and if the bright spot stay in his place, and spread not in the skin, but it be somewhat dark; it is a rising of the burning, and the priest shall pronounce him clean: for it is an inflammation of the burning' — if the spot stayed and did not spread, and was dim, it being only the rising (scar) of the burn, the inflammation, and the priest pronouncing clean (the stay and spread not, the marks of the harmless scar, vv.23, the rising of the burning, the burn's scar, not leprosy, the pronounce clean, the verdict of cleanness, the figure, the harmless mark of the old injury distinguished from the deadly plague, not every mark is leprosy); the spot not spreading (the harmless scar, vv.23) — the discernment (the harmless distinguished from the deadly); 'it is a rising of the burning, and the priest shall pronounce him clean: for it is an inflammation of the burning' (the burn's scar, not leprosy, the clean verdict, the figure, the harmless mark, the cleansing, the providence, the clean pronounced, the man restored) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And if the bright spot stay in his place, and spread not in the skin, but it be somewhat dark; it is a rising of the burning, and the priest shall pronounce him clean
the non-spreading burn pronounced clean
the harmless scar of the burn distinguished from the deadly plague (vv.23)
the clean not condemned, the careful discernment

schools.summary: The plague of the head or beard: 'if a man or woman have a plague upon the head or the beard' — the next case concerning a plague (a scall, a scaly eruption) on the head or beard, in the hair (the head or beard, the hairy places, the scall, the man or woman, both subject to the plague, the figure, the plague in the hair, the scall, a kind of leprosy of the head or beard, to be judged, vv.30, the corruption in the head, the seat of thought, or the beard, the mark of maturity); the plague on the head or beard (the scall, the scaly eruption in the hair) — the case (the scall to be judged, vv.30); 'if a man or woman' (both man and woman subject, the plague no respecter of persons, the figure, the corruption that may afflict any, the providence, the scall of the head or beard to be judged, vv.30-37) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: If a man or woman have a plague upon the head or the beard
the plague of the head or beard
the scall, a kind of leprosy in the hair of the head or beard
afflicting man or woman, to be judged by the priest

schools.summary: The scall judged to be leprosy: 'then the priest shall see the plague: and, behold, if it be in sight deeper than the skin; and there be in it a yellow thin hair; then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a dry scall, even a leprosy upon the head or beard' — if the scall was deeper than the skin and had a yellow thin hair, the priest pronouncing unclean — it was a dry scall, a leprosy of the head or beard (the deeper than the skin and yellow thin hair, the marks of the leprous scall, like the white hair and depth elsewhere, vv.3, the dry scall, the scaly leprosy of the hair, the pronounce unclean, the verdict, the figure, the leprosy of the head or beard, the corruption in the hair, the deep, settled plague); the marks of the leprous scall (deeper, yellow thin hair, like the marks elsewhere, vv.3) — the signs (the true plague of the head); 'pronounce him unclean: it is a dry scall, even a leprosy upon the head or beard' (the unclean verdict, the leprous scall, the figure, the leprosy of the head or beard, the corruption in the hair, the providence, the scall judged unclean, the further cases to follow, vv.31-37) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Then the priest shall see the plague: and, behold, if it be in sight deeper than the skin; and there be in it a yellow thin hair; then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a dry scall, even a leprosy upon the head or beard
